Name: Philipp Haidinger
Accident:
We took the first bus up to Monte Brento. On the way, we were checking the wind, and soon we saw that there was no wind at the top. We made the final decision to go for the Spartacus exit next to Monte Brento. This was the last exit he was missing from the top ones. He told me he really wanted to complete all the top exits.
We took a really nice hike up and went straight to the exit. When we arrived, we saw that the conditions were good: a soft breeze to no wind, with a slight north/east breeze. He felt really good (too good) at the exit and liked the place and the rock he would push off from. There was nothing he didn’t like about that jump.
He had watched a video on YouTube the day before showing how others flew the line. I told him many times not to fly the same line he had seen for the first time and to keep it high and safe. Fly straight and so soon you see that you have big margin turn left. He jumped first. His exit was solid strong push and quick inflation. He had a really good start and had good altitude to the terrain.
As he started flying, I saw that he didn’t go straight for the steeper part, which we had talked about. Instead, he turned soft left toward the flatter area where there is also a small hill. About ten seconds after the exit, I saw him softly touching a tree. From my perspective, he had good speed and didn’t lose altitude from my point of view… he was not close to a stall or anything like that. Maybe and probably he didn’t saw this dry tree.. this happened to him already few weeks back but he got away without anything. They are hard to spot in winter. However, his position became unstable, and from there he lost control and hit several more trees before impacting to the rocks. I have the camera and try to analyse what happen. If I‘m wrong I‘ll give an update.
In my eyes, he was overconfident and willing to try this line immediately. Going aggressive since the first second. Many of us and me had talked with him thousand times over the years about his jumping in overall. He had almost no wingsuit skydiving experience, he came to Brento and essentially did his first wingsuit jumps there, on his own terms. He pushed hard on every jump, not willing to learn a proper glide position or really understand how to fly. From jump to jump, there was only one way down low and fast. He had many close calls in his base career, willing to take a lot of risk even when he was alone. Sad but true
If I wouldn’t be with him he would be by his own thats how he was and he went for that what he wanted.
It’s sad but true: we lost a really humble, good, and kind soul. We all told him this would not end well, but he didn’t care that’s what he told us. He wanted that way.
Fly free, we love you Phil, aka Bumpsy
 
 
Updated 8th Jan 2026 
 
Update:
I was able to recover the video.
It happened as I explained before. He had a good start, began flying, and turned slightly to the left. He was aiming for that hill on the left that has a kind of tree gate at the end. He aimed and passed through that tree gate but didn’t see the dry tree afterward. He went that way to aggressive. He hit that tree on the side, not in the center.
After the impact he slightly lost control and speed. He kept flying for 2 seconds further till the next hit. Despite the hit, he would still have been high enough after the first hit to fly out if he hadn’t lost that much control and height. I know it doesn’t change anything, but I just wanted to say it.
What we can learn from this aside from the mental aspects he had is something we already know, but must take extra seriously in winter, early spring, and autumn after the leaves have fallen.
When flying a wingsuit above trees without leaves, you can visually see all the way down to the ground. This creates a dangerous visual illusion: it makes you believe you are flying higher than you actually are and that you can safely fly lower. In reality, the treetops without leaves are almost invisible.
In late spring and summer, the situation is different. The green leaves are clearly visible, and your brain interprets them as the “bottom” or a solid barrier (DANGER). As a result, you naturally keep more distance, fly higher, and avoid getting as close.
Trees without leaves can fool your sense of distance. Always keep extra margin.

Name: William Bidou
Accident:
This Thursday was a perfect day for William. A morning of ski touring with his girlfriend, followed by a small jump at the Criou to properly finish the last day of good weather before starting to work 7 days a week for the February holidays.
Despite having said the day before that he would never take her to the exit again, William managed to convince Amélie to drive him up to the Pertuet chalets in the 4x4. He then continued alone, calmly, sending us videos and calling a few friends—maybe to express the joy of living a perfect day?
Arriving around 2:20 p.m., he sent me a voice message and then a video of the exit he was clearing of snow. He took the time to remove all the snow from the rock, pissed on it to be sure, and was waiting for the rock to dry completely before jumping.When I spoke to him just before he jumped, he told me he was calmly smoking a cigarette and getting ready to jump in about fifteen minutes.That was the last exchange I had with him. He seemed peaceful, relaxed, and simply happy to be enjoying his day.
At 4:11 p.m., having heard nothing from him, Amélie—who was waiting for him down below—called me, and I then alerted the rescue services.The PGHM found William at the bottom of the cliff around 5:30 p.m., as if he had never even taken flight.
I went back up two days later and, despite a few centimeters of fresh snow, I found his tracks and the cleared exit.The rock from which we push off was solid and in place; only the small fir tree next to the rock seemed lower than usual—bent by the fresh snow or broken by William? I couldn’t get close enough to be sure.
William wasn’t wearing crampons. He always pushed hard at the exit. The most likely hypothesis is a slip—either while pushing or while moving toward the edge…
William was my partner, a friend like you only have a few of in a lifetime, a passionate guy who always excelled at everything he undertook. He always wanted to push the limits, especially in wingsuit flying, where he had an unfortunate tendency to get far too close to the terrain. Despite all the arguments we had about it, this story proves that even when you push too much in this sport, you can die from a stupid mistake…
I don’t know if there’s really any lesson to be learned from this BFL, other than that in winter there is snow—and snow without crampons is slippery…
This jump was our jump. We did it more than fifty times, mostly together, and every time we saw “the ibex ledge” a few dozen meters below the exit. I still can’t believe that it’s that ledge that probably cost you your life.You were my partner, my buddy, my colleague, my brother.I’m going to miss you a lot Gros PDI love youCe jeudi était une journée parfaite pour William. Matinée ski de rando avec sa copine suivie d'un petit saut au Criou bien finir la dernière journée de beau temps avant avant de commencer ton travail 7/7 pour les vacances de février.
Malgré son intention exprimée la veille de ne plus jamais l'emmener à l'exit, William a réussi à convaincre Amélie de le monter jusqu'aux chalets de Pertuet en 4x4. Puis il a continué à monter seul, tranquillement, en nous envoyant des vidéos et en téléphonant à quelques amis, peut être pour exprimer sa joie d'être entrain de vivre une journée parfaite?
Arrivé vers 14h20 il m'a envoyé un vocal puis une vidéo de l'exit qu'il était entrain de déneiger, il a pris le temps d'enlever toute la neige du caillou, a pissé dessus pour être sûr et était entrain d'attendre que le rocher sèche totalement avant de sauter.
Lorsque je l'ai eu juste avant qu'il saute, il m'a dit être tranquillement entrain de fumer une clope et de se préparer pour sauter dans une quinzaine de minutes.
C'est le dernier échange que j'ai eu avec lui, il semblait apaisé, détendu et juste heureux de profiter sa journée.
À 16h11, n'ayant pas de ses nouvelles, Amélie qui l'attendait en bas m'a appelé, puis j'ai déclanché les secours.
Le PGHM a retrouvé William en bas de la falaise vers 17h30, comme s'il ne s'était jamais mis en vol.
Je suis monté le surlendemain et malgré les quelques centimètres de neige fraîche j'ai trouvé ses traces et son déneigement de l'exit.
Le rocher depuis lequel on pousse était bien en place, seulement le petit sapin bordant se caillou semblait plus bas que d'habitude, plié par la neige fraîche ou cassé par William? Je n'ai pas pu m'approcher assez pour en être sûr.
William n'avait pas ses crampons, il poussait toujours fort à l'exit. L'hypothèse la plus probable est celle d'une zipette, soit en poussant soit en allant au bord du trou...
William était mon binôme, un ami comme on en a moins que les doigts d'une main, un passionné qui a toujours performé dans tout ce qu'il a entrepris. Il a toujours voulu repousser les limites notamment en wingsuit où il avait une fâcheuse tendance à se rapprocher bien trop près du relief. Malgré toutes les engueulades qu'on a eues à ce sujet, l'histoire nous a prouvé que même lorsqu'on engage trop dans ce sport, on peut mourir d'une erreur bête...
Je sais pas s'il y a vraiment de leçon à apprendre de cette BFL mis à part que l'hiver il y a de la neige et que la neige sans crampons ça glisse...
Ce saut c'était notre saut, on l'a fait plus d'une cinquantaine de fois, majoritairement ensemble et à chaque fois on voyait "la vire des bouquetinos" quelques dizaines de mètres sous l'exit, j'ai encore du mal à croire que c'est cette vire qui t'a certainement coûté la vie.
Tu étais mon binôme, mon pote, mon collègue, mon frère
Tu vas me manquer Gros PD
Je t'aime
